text: The laparoscopic procedure involves the resection of Meckel's diverticulum as part of addressing small bowel volvulus, with visualization of the affected bowel segment and surrounding structures.
A thirty-five year old man presented with central abdominal pain and vomiting.
A similar episode six months previously resolved spontaneously.
On examination the abdomen was mildly distended and tender in the right iliac fossa.
A CT scan showed a small bowel obstruction and was suggestive of a Meckel's diverticulum.
Following resuscitation, the patient was brought to the operating theatre for a laparoscopy.
The surgeon identifies a Meckel's diverticulum with a persistent vitelline ligament attached to the abdominal wall at the umbilicus, serving as the rotation point for a small bowel volvulus.
The surgeon observes a segment of the small bowel that appears engorged, with a noticeable increase in size and fullness, but without signs of ischemia such as discoloration or necrosis.
The surgeon uses atraumatic graspers to reduce the segment of small bowel involved in the volvulus, carefully manipulating the bowel to alleviate the obstruction.

The surgeon performs a thorough examination of the small bowel, visually inspecting the entire length from the duodenal jejunal ligament to the ileocecal valve to ensure no additional pathology is present.
The surgeon observes the small bowel mucosa, which is visibly constricted following the reduction of the volvulus.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the vitiline ligament at its attachment to the abdominal wall, addressing the point of rotation for the small bowel volvulus.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to ligate and divide the persistent vitiline artery supplying the Meckel's diverticulum, ensuring the artery is no longer supplying blood to the diverticulum.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapling device to resect the Meckel's diverticulum, ensuring the lumen of the ileum remains uncompromised during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic retrieval bag to extract the resected specimen through a ten millimeter port.
The patient made an uneventful post-operative recovery and was discharged home on the second post-operative day.
